target.title: feat(Signal): support note-to-self ingress
target.body:
## Summary

### What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds explicit Signal `ingressMode: "note-to-self"` support so linked-device `signal-cli` setups can use the operator's Signal Note to Self chat as the OpenClaw conversation surface.
- Removes the practical need to buy, register, and maintain a second phone number and Signal account just to run OpenClaw as a personal Signal bot.
- Promotes only self-addressed `syncMessage.sentMessage` envelopes into the normal inbound pipeline, while continuing to drop sent transcripts addressed to other people and direct own-account non-sync echoes.
- Adds self-reply echo suppression so OpenClaw replies sent into Note to Self do not re-enter as fresh prompts.

### Why does this matter now?

- Today, a reliable Signal setup effectively assumes a dedicated bot number/account. That is a high-friction requirement for the common personal use case where the operator only wants to message their own OpenClaw instance through Signal, not expose OpenClaw to contacts.
- Signal Note to Self is the natural secure chat surface for that use case, and linked-device Note to Self messages arrive as sent-sync events that need explicit, self-only handling before agent dispatch.
- This PR makes that behavior explicit and covered across opt-in config, UUID handling, attachments, edits, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, and self-reply loop protection.

### What is the intended outcome?

- Existing `standard` Signal deployments keep their current behavior.
- Users who opt into `channels.signal.ingressMode="note-to-self"` can send text, attachments, edits, and approval reactions through their own Signal Note to Self chat.
- OpenClaw ignores sent-sync transcripts to other Signal recipients and avoids looping on its own Note to Self replies.
- After community testing and follow-up hardening, this mode could become the default-recommended Signal gateway setup for personal deployments in a future PR.

### What is intentionally out of scope?

- No global/core `dmPolicy` changes.
- No change to the default-recommended Signal setup in this PR.
- No required `accountUuid` config for normal users; UUID discovery reads the local signal-cli account store instead.
- No durable persistence for Note to Self self-reply echo markers across gateway restarts; replay suppression is intentionally process-local.

### What does success look like?

- Focused Signal tests pass for account discovery, Note to Self sync promotion, UUID-only routing, edits, attachment-only messages, approval reactions, echo suppression, and legacy approval-reaction key compatibility.
- A real linked-device Signal setup can process phone-originated Note to Self text, image-only, edited-message, approval-reaction, and agent-driven reaction action flows without observed self-reply loops.

### What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `ingressMode="note-to-self"` is bounded to Signal and remains opt-in.
- Whether self-addressed sent-sync promotion cannot accidentally process transcripts to other recipients.
- Whether echo suppression is strong enough without adding persistent state.
- Whether UUID normalization/discovery and approval reaction matching preserve existing behavior.

## Linked context

### Which issue does this close?

Closes #75870.

### Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Supersedes and extends #75890.

Related #5722.

### Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request cited; this follows the linked issue and earlier PR context.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Linked-device Signal deployments can route user-authored Note to Self messages into OpenClaw. The same mode must keep ignoring sent transcripts to other contacts and must not loop when OpenClaw's own replies or reactions sync back through Signal.
- Real environment tested: Focused local Vitest coverage and branch autoreview ran in the OpenClaw checkout. Live proof ran against a real linked-device Signal setup using `signal-cli 0.14.3`, an installed local OpenClaw build from this branch, and `channels.signal.ingressMode="note-to-self"` with `receiveMode="on-start"`, `dmPolicy="disabled"`, and `groupPolicy="disabled"`. Agent-driven Signal reaction-action proof also used `tools.profile="messaging"`, because the `coding` profile intentionally removes the `message` tool.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Local verification commands were `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/account-store.test.ts extensions/signal/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-auth.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/signal/src/message-actions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.inbound-context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts extensions/signal/src/normalize.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send.test.ts extensions/signal/src/self-reply-echoes.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`, `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`, `pnpm tsgo:prod`, `pnpm check:test-types`, `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`, `pnpm config:channels:check`, `git diff --check`, and `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`. Packaging and install proof was `pnpm build`, `npm pack`, install the packed local build, `openclaw gateway restart`, `openclaw status --deep`, and `openclaw logs --limit 80 --plain`. Live Signal proof covered these scenarios: phone-originated Note to Self text prompt requesting a unique reply token; phone-originated attachment-only Note to Self image prompt; edited Note to Self prompt changing the requested reply token; `/diagnostics note-to-self approval reaction test` approved by reacting `👍` in Signal; installed local build retested with a fresh Note to Self text prompt; inbound normal DM from another Signal account to the configured Signal number; outbound normal DM from the configured Signal account to another Signal account; direct CLI reaction delivery with `openclaw message react --channel signal --target <redacted-own-signal-account> --message-id <redacted-message-id> --emoji "👀" --json`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction add after `openclaw config set tools.profile messaging` and `openclaw gateway restart`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction add for `✅`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction removal for `✅`.
- Evidence: Unit and integration tests cover Note to Self sent-sync promotion, third-party sent transcript drops, UUID-only paths, edits, attachment-only messages, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, account UUID discovery, authoritative configured signal-cli `dataDir` handling, outbound echo recording, timestamp-less and timestamped media fallback suppression, reaction echo suppression, reaction response-loss ambiguity handling, default-account UUID inheritance/deletion, UUID-preserving allowlists, and legacy approval-reaction key compatibility. Live proof showed Signal channel startup and reply delivery in the gateway log:

```text
channels/signal [default] starting provider (http://127.0.0.1:8080)
signal-cli: INFO HttpServerHandler - Started HTTP server on /127.0.0.1:8080
channels/signal delivered reply to <redacted-own-signal-account>
```

- Observed result: Local verification passed: the focused Signal suite passed with 156 tests, channel config check passed, bundled-extension lint passed, production type checks passed, test type checks passed, package-boundary compile passed, whitespace checks passed, and autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings. Gateway proof passed: `openclaw status --deep` reported `Signal ON OK configured` on the installed local build. Note to Self text proof passed: a phone-originated Note to Self prompt produced the requested unique token. Note to Self media proof passed: an attachment-only image prompt reached a vision-capable model and the reply described the image text. Note to Self edit proof passed: editing the original Signal message produced a second reply with the edited requested token. Approval reaction proof passed: reacting `👍` to the diagnostics approval prompt resolved the exec approval exactly once with `Exec approval allowed once. Resolved by Signal approval (<redacted-own-signal-account>).` Third-party inbound DM isolation passed: a normal DM from another Signal account arrived on the configured Signal account's phone and linked signal-cli instance, but OpenClaw did not reply, did not send a pairing request, and did not appear to start an agent turn. Third-party outbound sent-sync isolation passed: a normal DM sent from the configured Signal account to another Signal account appeared in signal-cli and on both phones as a regular Signal message, but OpenClaw did not reply or otherwise act on that sent-sync transcript. Direct reaction delivery passed: CLI reaction delivery returned `handledBy: "plugin"`, `ok: true`, and `added: "👀"`. Agent-driven reaction proof passed: with `tools.profile="messaging"`, the agent used the `message` tool to add `👀`, used the `message` tool to add `✅`, then used the `message` tool to remove `✅`. No self-reply loop or reaction-triggered loop was observed in any live proof path.
- What was not tested: Broader Signal device/version coverage is left to CI and maintainer/community testing. Agent-driven reaction actions were live-tested only after enabling the `messaging` tool profile; with `tools.profile="coding"`, the `message` tool is intentionally unavailable and the model replied with text instead of sending a reaction. A daemon-originated self-send was tested and confirmed not to emit a receive event on `/api/v1/events`; the successful Note to Self live proof used the primary phone app, which is the real user path.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Live Signal proof used one local linked-device Signal setup with redacted account identifiers. It proves the intended personal Note to Self path and the tested isolation cases, not every Signal client/device version.

## Tests and validation

### Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/account-store.test.ts extensions/signal/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-auth.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/signal/src/message-actions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.inbound-context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts extensions/signal/src/normalize.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send.test.ts extensions/signal/src/self-reply-echoes.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`

### What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Account UUID discovery from signal-cli config/account-store layouts.
- Authoritative configured signal-cli `dataDir` handling, including stale and ambiguous configured stores that fail closed instead of falling back to a different default profile.
- Parse-compatible `accountUuid` handling for shipped string configs, with runtime UUID normalization for bare, compact, and `uuid:<id>` forms.
- Note to Self sync-message promotion for text, nested messages, edits, and attachment-only messages.
- Third-party sent transcript drops for phone and UUID destination mismatches.
- Self-reply echo suppression for timestamp IDs, timestamp-less text/media fallbacks, timestamped media exact/generic fallbacks, provisional pre-send guards, reaction echoes, ambiguous response loss, and late UUID discovery.
- Signal approval reaction matching across phone, compact UUID, hyphenated UUID, and legacy persisted reaction keys.
- Default/named account inheritance behavior for Note to Self mode and `accountUuid`.
- Agent-driven Signal reaction actions, including forwarding resolved account, UUID, and signal-cli config path context into reaction sends.
- UUID-preserving Signal allowlist/security normalization for `uuid:<id>` entries.

### What behavior does this coverage protect?

- Linked-device Note to Self prompts arrive as `syncMessage.sentMessage`, so current behavior needs a self-only path into agent dispatch.
- Note to Self mode needs coverage for UUID identity, attachments, edits, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, third-party transcript drops, and self-reply echoes.

### If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added and live behavior was verified.

## Risk checklist

### Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Signal users can opt into a new `note-to-self` ingress mode.

### Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This adds `channels.signal.ingressMode` and documents optional `channels.signal.accountUuid`, plus generated channel config metadata and docs. Existing configs keep the default `standard` mode; `accountUuid` remains parse-compatible with shipped string configs and only valid UUID forms are used for runtime identity matching.

### Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, bounded to Signal channel ingress and native approval reaction handling. There are no new external network calls or provider/tool permissions.

### What is the highest-risk area?

- Accidentally treating sent transcripts to other Signal recipients as user prompts, or letting OpenClaw's own Note to Self replies loop back into the agent.

### How is that risk mitigated?

- The mode is explicit opt-in and Signal-local.
- Self-addressed sent-sync promotion checks source/destination identity, including UUID-only envelopes and valid runtime UUID normalization.
- Sent transcripts to other recipients are dropped before dispatch.
- Self-reply echo suppression records outbound replies, media fallbacks, and reaction fingerprints in process memory under the effective Signal phone/UUID identities.
- Focused tests cover destination mismatch drops, UUID normalization, edits, attachments, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, and echo suppression.

## Current review state

### What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review.

### What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Nothing known from the author side. CI and maintainer review are still needed after PR creation.

### Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Local branch autoreview reports no accepted/actionable findings. ClawSweeper's `accountUuid` compatibility review is addressed by permissive schema parsing with runtime UUID normalization.

## Notes For Maintainers

- This intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`; release-owned changelog generation should pick up the PR/commit context.
- `channels.signal.accountUuid` intentionally remains parse-compatible with shipped string configs. Runtime identity matching only treats valid bare, compact, or legacy `uuid:<id>` UUID forms as UUIDs; docs recommend bare UUIDs and normal users should rely on auto-discovery instead.
- If a maintainer wants live Signal proof before merge, the code is structured so the requested scenario can be demonstrated without changing the PR: configure `ingressMode: "note-to-self"`, start the Signal monitor, and send a Note to Self message from the primary device.

target.title: test: stabilize maintainer prepare baseline
target.body:
## Summary

Stabilizes the local maintainer prepare baseline failures seen while preparing #85791.

- updates the node-host allow-always coverage test to use a supported direct executable fixture instead of a shell-wrapped interpreter command that is now rejected by the safety boundary
- skips the chmod cleanup retry assertion under root, where root can remove through the permission-denied fixture
- isolates mac packaging PATH fixtures from host `/usr/bin/pnpm` / `/usr/bin/corepack`
- makes Telegram channel-post album timer tests deterministic and fail loudly on timeout
- increases Slack dropped-history media resolution timeout so WSL/root lanes do not miss delayed history media

## Maintainer alert

This does not add config schema/options, plugin API/contract, CLI flags, env vars, migrations, or public config/plugin surface.

It does intentionally change Slack dropped-history media behavior by increasing the internal media resolution budget from 1s/3s to 10s/15s. That is not a new surface, but it is maintainer-visible channel behavior and should be accepted explicitly before merge.

target.title: Support compat reasoning levels for thinking xhigh
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Allows OpenAI-compatible reasoning models to expose `xhigh` thinking when the model configuration explicitly declares API support through `compat.supportedReasoningEfforts`.
- Keeps `xhigh` hidden for reasoning models that do not declare extended compatible reasoning effort support.
- Keeps `max` conservative: it is surfaced only when compat metadata explicitly supports `max` (for example `reasoningEffortMap.max`), while plain `supportedReasoningEfforts: ["xhigh"]` surfaces only `xhigh`.

Why does this matter now?

- Some OpenAI-compatible providers support reasoning efforts beyond OpenAI's standard `minimal`/`low`/`medium`/`high` set, but OpenClaw's UI/runtime capability calculation filtered those levels out before consulting explicit compat support.
- That makes valid configured models look like they cannot use `xhigh`, even when the provider explicitly lists that effort in compat metadata.

What is the intended outcome?

- Configured compatible models can advertise and use `xhigh` only when `compat.supportedReasoningEfforts` explicitly includes it, and can advertise `max` only when explicit compat metadata says `max` is supported.
- Default reasoning-model behavior remains conservative for providers without explicit extended effort support.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No new config fields.
- No change to model definitions that do not opt into extended compatible reasoning efforts.
- No local installed-runtime hotfix; this is the durable source-level change.

What does success look like?

- Existing OpenAI reasoning models keep the previous supported thinking levels.
- OpenAI-compatible models with explicit `supportedReasoningEfforts` can surface `xhigh`; `reasoningEffortMap` can map supported levels but does not by itself expose new extended levels; `max` requires explicit `max` support in compat metadata.
- OpenAI-compatible models without explicit extended effort support still do not surface `xhigh`.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The compatibility gate around extended reasoning efforts.
- Whether `max` is gated strictly enough for compatible-provider reasoning effort checks.
- Regression coverage in `src/llm/model-utils.test.ts`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #59416
Related #74273
Related #84902

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer request from me; this is an external contributor fix for behavior observed in a real OpenClaw runtime using an OpenAI-compatible reasoning model.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI-compatible reasoning models with explicit compat metadata for extended reasoning efforts were not allowed to expose `xhigh` thinking, and `max` needed an explicit opt-in path.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw runtime on Linux, current session using model `codex-lb-2455/gpt-5.5` with `Think: xhigh` visible in `session_status` output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `session_status(model="", sessionKey="current")`
  - Focused source tests and changed-file checks listed below.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - `session_status` output included: `OpenClaw 2026.6.1`, `Model: codex-lb-2455/gpt-5.5`, and `Think: xhigh`.
  - Targeted regression tests in `src/llm/model-utils.test.ts` pass locally.
- Observed result after fix: Compatible reasoning-level capability calculation now includes `xhigh` for explicitly opted-in compatible models, includes `max` only with explicit `max` support, and keeps non-opted-in models conservative.
- What was not tested: The repository's delegated Blacksmith Testbox changed-file gate could not complete from this host because the Blacksmith CLI is not authenticated to an organization.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Blacksmith browser auth completed to a `No Organizations Found` page because this GitHub account has no organization with the Blacksmith GitHub App installed. `blacksmith auth status` reports `Not authenticated to any organization. Run 'blacksmith auth login' to get started.`
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The original runtime issue was that `xhigh` was not surfaced for an OpenAI-compatible reasoning model even though compat metadata mapped/supported the provider effort.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/llm/model-utils.ts src/llm/model-utils.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint src/llm/model-utils.ts src/llm/model-utils.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/model-utils.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:test:src
pnpm check:changed -- src/llm/model-utils.ts src/llm/model-utils.test.ts
blacksmith auth status || true
```

Results:

- `oxfmt`: passed, all matched files use the correct format.
- `oxlint`: passed, 0 warnings and 0 errors.
- Targeted Vitest: passed, `src/llm/model-utils.test.ts`, 4 tests / 4 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`: passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`: passed.
- `pnpm check:changed`: blocked by Blacksmith CLI auth/org infrastructure, not by a code/test failure:

```text
[check:changed] delegating to Blacksmith Testbox via `pnpm crabbox:run`.
[crabbox] bin=../crabbox/bin/crabbox version=0.26.0-1-gc6fef7c provider=blacksmith-testbox providers=apple-container,ascii-box,aws,azure,azure-dynamic-sessions,blacksmith-testbox,cloudflare,daytona,e2b,exe-dev,gcp,hetzner,islo,local-container,modal,multipass,namespace-devbox,parallels,proxmox,railway,runpod,semaphore,sprites,ssh,tensorlake,upstash-box,wandb
Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
blacksmith testbox warmup failed: blacksmith failed: exit status 1; if the delegated queue is unavailable, rerun with a coordinator-backed provider such as --provider aws
```

`blacksmith auth status`:

```text
Not authenticated to any organization. Run 'blacksmith auth login' to get started.
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added tests for OpenAI-compatible reasoning models that explicitly support `xhigh` via `compat.supportedReasoningEfforts`.
- Added tests for conservative `compat.reasoningEffortMap` / `max` alias behavior, including map-only aliases that must not expose new extended levels.
- Added tests that reasoning models without explicit extended compat support still do not expose `xhigh`.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Capability calculation filtered out `xhigh`/`max` for compatible reasoning models before explicit compat reasoning effort support was considered; the follow-up also prevents map-only aliases from exposing new extended levels.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, for explicitly configured OpenAI-compatible reasoning models only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Accidentally exposing unsupported thinking levels for providers that do not really accept extended reasoning efforts.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The new behavior is gated on explicit compat support (`supportedReasoningEfforts` / `reasoningEffortMap`).
- Regression tests cover both opted-in and non-opted-in reasoning models.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review. GitHub CI is green; delegated local Blacksmith Testbox validation remains unavailable in this external contributor environment.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- The external contributor environment cannot complete the delegated `pnpm check:changed` Blacksmith Testbox gate because Blacksmith auth has no available organization. GitHub CI for the PR is green.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- claw-sweeper's `max`/`xhigh` compatibility concern was addressed in follow-up commit `e8292687` by requiring explicit `max` compat metadata before surfacing `max`.

target.title: fix(agents): reconnect streamable-HTTP MCP sessions on session loss [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a remote streamable-HTTP MCP server restarts, it drops the in-memory session it handed us. OpenClaw keeps using the stale session id, so every subsequent `callTool` fails — HTTP `404` (the MCP-spec session-terminated signal) or a `-32603 "Session not found"` body — until the whole agent session is reset. In practice this silently breaks an agent's MCP tools mid-conversation whenever the remote bounces.
- **Fix:** `callTool` now detects the lost-session signal, re-handshakes the server once on a fresh client, and retries the call on the new session.
- **Outcome:** MCP tools self-heal after a remote restart instead of failing every call until the session is reset.
- **Out of scope:** Only `callTool` is auto-healed (the hot path); `listResources` / `readResource` / `listPrompts` / `getPrompt` are unchanged (a subsequent `callTool` re-establishes the session anyway). Reconnect is gated to the `streamable-http` transport — stdio/SSE do not have this remote-session-drop failure mode.
- **Reviewer focus:** the `callTool` retry placement inside `runGuardedServerRequest` (a healed reconnect clears the backoff; a real failure still records one), and the loop/dedupe/leak guarantees in `reconnectSession`.

## Linked context

No existing issue — confirmed via issue/PR search (`MCP session not found`, `streamable session reconnect`); happy to open one if preferred.

Maintainer/owner requested: no — community contribution.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** a self-hosted streamable-HTTP MCP server that periodically restarts; afterward every tool call against it returned `-32603 "Session not found"` until the agent session was reset.
- **Real environment tested:** a self-hosted production OpenClaw gateway (Docker), running the functionally identical fix.
- **Exact steps:** deployed the patched runtime; confirmed the gateway came back healthy; exercised the MCP-backed scheduled job that reads from the remote MCP server and then delivers the result.
- **Evidence after fix:** the scheduled run completed with `status: ok` and `delivered: true` and a payload fully sourced from live MCP reads — i.e. MCP reads succeed end-to-end post-fix (captured from the gateway's cron run-history).
- **Observed result after fix:** MCP-backed runs succeed instead of failing on a stale session.
- **What was not tested live:** forcing an actual mid-session remote restart in production to watch the reconnect+retry fire. That specific path is covered by the new deterministic tests (the mocked transport drops the session, and the tests assert exactly one reconnect + retry, dedupe of concurrent reconnects, no retry storm, and no client leak).
- **Proof limitations / constraints:** my checkout was behind `main`, so the deployed build carries the identical fix on the pre-rename file (`pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`); this PR ports it onto the current `agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`. The host is Docker-only and could not install the v2026.6.2 toolchain to run the full gate locally without risking the production box, so `pnpm tsgo` / `pnpm test` / `pnpm build` are left to CI on this branch.

## Tests and validation

- **Commands run:** `oxfmt --check` (clean on both changed files); the equivalent reconnect suite (same test logic, pre-rename module) ran green on the deployed build — 13 reconnect cases plus the existing 6-case runtime suite, with tsgo / oxfmt / oxlint / build all clean. The SDK contracts this relies on (`ErrorCode.InternalError === -32603`, `StreamableHTTPError` constructor + `.code`) were verified against the version-matched SDK `1.29.0`.
- **Regression coverage added:** `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.reconnect.test.ts` — the `isMcpSessionLostError` detector (404, leaked `Session not found` body, HTTP-200 `McpError`-shaped `-32603`, and negatives) plus the reconnect / retry / catalog-stability / no-loop / non-streamable-gate / connect-failure / concurrent-dedupe / mid-reconnect-disposal paths.
- **What failed before:** every `callTool` against the server after the remote dropped its session.
- **Full tsgo/test/build:** deferred to CI for the reasons under proof limitations.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — MCP tool calls recover after a remote restart instead of failing until session reset.
- **Config / environment / migration change?** No.
- **Security / auth / secrets / network / tool-execution change?** Reconnect re-establishes the MCP transport from the same already-resolved server config; no new auth/secret handling. It performs at most one extra handshake + one retry per failed call.
- **Highest-risk area:** an unbounded reconnect loop, or a leaked half-open client.
- **Mitigation:** at most one reconnect + one retry per call (the retry is not re-wrapped, so a flapping server cannot loop); a per-server in-flight map dedupes concurrent reconnects; the reconnect path disposes the fresh client on connect failure or mid-reconnect disposal. Each of these has a dedicated test.

## Current review state

Next action: CI run + maintainer review.

target.title: fix(amazon-bedrock): skip IMDS during credential auto-detection (#64891)
target.body:
﻿## Summary

- Stop Bedrock memory auto-detection from probing AWS IMDS (`169.254.169.254`) when no AWS credential signals are configured.
- Keep the AWS SDK default credential chain available for explicitly selected/configured Bedrock memory providers, including shared config/profile, SSO, web identity, ECS, and EC2/IMDS role setups.
- During non-IMDS profile/container/web-identity probes, temporarily set `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true` so `defaultProvider()` skips instance metadata while still resolving configured non-IMDS sources.
- Memoize `process.env` credential probes for 5 minutes and coalesce concurrent calls without sharing cached results across IMDS-enabled and IMDS-disabled probe modes.

Closes #64891

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Bedrock memory auto-detection no longer loads the AWS SDK credential chain, and therefore does not reach IMDS, when there are no AWS credential signals. Explicit Bedrock memory configuration still allows the full AWS SDK default chain.

Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw source checkout, running the patched Amazon Bedrock provider code directly through Node 22/tsx with AWS credential environment variables cleared.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```powershell
node --import tsx -e "const awsKeys=['AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID','AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY','AWS_SESSION_TOKEN','AWS_PROFILE','AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK','AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI','AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_FULL_URI','AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE','AWS_ROLE_ARN','AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED']; for (const key of awsKeys) delete process.env[key]; const { hasAwsCredentials } = await import('./extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.ts'); const started=Date.now(); const result=await hasAwsCredentials(); console.log(JSON.stringify({setup:'local OpenClaw source checkout',command:'hasAwsCredentials() with AWS credential env cleared',awsCredentialEnvPresent:awsKeys.filter((key)=>process.env[key]),result,elapsedMs:Date.now()-started,metadataDisabledAfter:process.env.AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED ?? null},null,2));"
```

Evidence after fix: Console output from the local OpenClaw source checkout:

```json
{
  "setup": "local OpenClaw source checkout",
  "command": "hasAwsCredentials() with AWS credential env cleared",
  "awsCredentialEnvPresent": [],
  "result": false,
  "elapsedMs": 0,
  "metadataDisabledAfter": null
}
```

Observed result after fix: With AWS credential environment cleared, the real provider code returned `false` immediately from the no-signal auto-detection path. The command completed without setting `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED` and without waiting on the AWS SDK metadata timeout path.

What was not tested: A live AWS EC2/ECS/default-chain credential setup and the original non-AWS sandbox packet trace were not available in this environment; those remain maintainer/live-environment proof gaps.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.test.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsgo -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --pretty false`
- `pnpm exec tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json --pretty false`
- `OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=1 pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`
- `OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=1 pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:canary`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.test.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Test plan

- [x] Empty env skips credential SDK load
- [x] `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true` exits without SDK when no probe signals
- [x] `AWS_PROFILE` probe disables IMDS temporarily and restores env
- [x] Explicit Bedrock provider/config preserves full AWS SDK default chain probing
- [x] `process.env` probes are memoized and coalesced per IMDS mode
- [ ] Maintainer/live env: non-AWS sandbox startup shows no repeated `169.254.169.254` traffic
- [ ] Maintainer/live env: EC2/ECS/default-chain Bedrock memory credentials still resolve

commands.author: Pick-cat

target.title: fix(agents): bound body-less MCP HTTP text responses
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`ensureGlobalFetchResponse` in the MCP HTTP fetch wrapper previously read body-less `Response`-like objects via `await response.text()`. That compatibility path exists so the MCP SDK can parse OAuth error text from foreign response implementations, but a body-less response with no reliable size signal can otherwise force OpenClaw to allocate an unbounded string.

This PR makes that body-less fallback policy explicit: text-only foreign responses are preserved only when they advertise a safe decimal `Content-Length`; missing, malformed, invalid, or oversized lengths fail closed before calling `text()`.

## Changes

- `src/agents/mcp-http-fetch.ts`
  - Adds `MCP_HTTP_MAX_TEXT_RESPONSE_BYTES = 1 * 1024 * 1024`.
  - Parses `Content-Length` for body-less foreign responses.
  - Accepts only trimmed decimal digit `Content-Length` values before converting to a safe integer.
  - Returns `new Response(null, init)` without calling `response.text()` when `Content-Length` is missing, malformed, invalid, negative, or over the cap.
  - Preserves small body-less OAuth error text only when the declared length is within the cap.
  - Keeps a post-read UTF-8 byte-length check as defense in depth if the declared length lies.
- `src/agents/mcp-http-fetch.test.ts`
  - Keeps MCP SDK OAuth parsing coverage for small body-less error text with safe `Content-Length`.
  - Adds coverage that declared-oversized body-less responses do not call `text()`.
  - Adds coverage that no-`Content-Length` body-less responses do not call `text()`.
  - Adds coverage that malformed non-decimal `Content-Length` values (`1e3`, `0x40`) do not call `text()`.
  - Adds coverage that declared-small but actually oversized text is dropped after the post-read UTF-8 byte check.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A body-less MCP HTTP foreign response with missing, malformed, or oversized `Content-Length` must not call `text()` at all; the wrapper should preserve status/headers and return `body=null`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Node v22 workspace, real `http.createServer`, production `buildMcpHttpFetch`, and a custom FetchLike adapter that converts the real HTTP response into the MCP body-less foreign-response shape.
- **Exact steps**: A local HTTP server returned a 1 MiB + 1 byte body. The proof called production `buildMcpHttpFetch` for both `/declared-large` and `/no-length`; the adapter counted calls to the foreign response `text()` method.
- **Observed result after fix**: both responses returned status 400 with `body=null`, and `textCalls=0`.
- **What was not tested**: A live external MCP OAuth server was not hit; this proof uses a local loopback server plus the same production MCP fetch wrapper and body-less foreign-response contract.

Loopback proof output:

```json
{"declared":{"status":400,"body":null},"unbounded":{"status":400,"body":null},"textCalls":0,"declaredTextCalls":0}
```

## Current status proof

- **Current head**: `8bb30cd79ae192d15c86deb10165d12ba450e8ca`.
- **Current GitHub PR diff**: `+160/-2 (2 files)`.
- **Current changed files**:

```text
src/agents/mcp-http-fetch.test.ts
src/agents/mcp-http-fetch.ts
```

- **Malformed length proof added**: focused regression coverage now proves `Content-Length: 1e3` and `Content-Length: 0x40` return `body=null` without calling `text()`.

## Compatibility / Policy Note

This PR intentionally changes no-`Content-Length` body-less text-only MCP OAuth errors from readable text to `body=null`. That is the fail-closed option from #97521. Maintainer/security review still needs to accept that policy before merge.

For declared-small body-less responses, the only available API is `text()`, so the implementation trusts a syntactically valid decimal `Content-Length` before reading and then verifies the returned UTF-8 byte length. The regression test covers a lying response that declares `Content-Length: 64` but returns more than 1 MiB; the wrapper drops it after the post-read check. Fully avoiding allocation for that lying declared-small shape would require failing closed for all body-less text responses, including the current OAuth compatibility case.

## Evidence

Focused Vitest after rejecting malformed `Content-Length` values:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/mcp-http-fetch.test.ts --run

Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

Whitespace/check sanity:

```text
$ git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
# exit 0
```

Earlier type checks on this PR:

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core      # exit 0
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test # exit 0
```

commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review remains because contributor proof is missing and the Matrix helper API/deprecation direction is a maintainer decision, not a safe automated repair target.

target.title: fix(matrix): add async shared-client stop helpers awaiting final persist
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The synchronous `stopSharedClient`, `stopSharedClientForAccount`, and `stopSharedClientInstance` helpers in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts` kick off the final crypto/sync persist via `MatrixClient.stop()` but return before it completes, so a process exit right after a stop can lose recently negotiated Olm/Megolm session keys.
- Why it matters: Lost crypto state shows up after restart as `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops and repeated "Established new Olm sessions" against the same sender.
- What changed: Added async `stopSharedClientAsync`, `stopSharedClientForAccountAsync`, and `stopSharedClientInstanceAsync` that await `client.stopAndPersist()`. Marked the legacy synchronous helpers `@deprecated`. Re-exported the two account-scoped async helpers from the matrix barrel. Added three Vitest cases. Added one CHANGELOG line.
- What did NOT change: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts` was not touched. `MatrixClient.stop()` already triggers the final persist and `stopAndPersist()` already exists; the periodic 60s timer and the four crypto-event bindings at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/decrypt-bridge.ts:153-164` were already wired.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #76611 
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: this PR narrowly fixes the synchronous stop-helper persistence defect from #76611 only. The event-driven crypto-state persist defect (`KeyBackupDecryptionKeyCached`, `RehydrationCompleted`, `DevicesUpdated`, `KeysChanged` not driving `persistIdbToDisk`) remains open and is not addressed here.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Sync helpers return `void` and call `client.stop()`, which assigns `stopPersistPromise` for the final IDB/sync flush but does not return it.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test pinned that shared-client teardown produces a settled persist promise; no `@deprecated` signal steered callers toward the existing async path.
- Contributing context: `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` and `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` already exist as the safe path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario: Each new async helper invokes `client.stopAndPersist()` exactly once on the targeted client(s) and does not call `client.stop()`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The shared-client registry is the single chokepoint between callers and `MatrixClient`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None for the async-stop helpers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None for end users. Plugin authors who deep-import `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client.ts` get three new async exports; legacy synchronous exports keep their current signatures.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Net effect on security posture is positive: hardens the Matrix E2EE crypto-store shutdown path so recently negotiated session keys are durable across restarts when the new helpers are adopted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11; fix is platform-agnostic.
- Runtime: Node 22.
- Integration: Matrix (`extensions/matrix`).

### Steps

1. Acquire a shared Matrix client through `resolveSharedMatrixClient` for an E2EE-enabled account.
2. Trigger a shutdown path that calls a synchronous `stopSharedClient*` helper and exits the process immediately.
3. Restart and observe whether incoming E2EE messages decrypt cleanly.

### Expected

- Recently negotiated Olm/Megolm sessions remain decryptable across restart.

### Actual (without this fix)

- Process exits before `stopPersistPromise` resolves; restart loses recent session keys, producing `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Three Vitest cases added in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`. CI must run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed`.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Static review against the existing `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` precedent at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts:295`. Verified the new helpers reuse `deleteSharedClientState` and `removeSharedClientInstance` with the same ordering as the existing async-release path. Verified the mock client in `shared.test.ts` exposes `stopAndPersist` without breaking the existing release-lease test.
- Edge cases checked: Concurrent acquire after a stop drops cleanly because state is removed before the awaited persist. `Promise.all` was chosen for `stopSharedClientAsync` to match the existing error semantics; inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)` at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts:677-689`.
- What was not verified: Vitest was not executed in the authoring shell (no `pnpm`/`node` on PATH). The reporter's full prod symptom was not reproduced against a live homeserver.

## Review Conversations

- [x] Bot review conversations addressed in this PR will be replied to or resolved.
- [x] Only conversations that still need maintainer judgment will be left open.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A future contributor copies a `@deprecated` synchronous helper into a new shutdown path despite the jsdoc warning.
  - Mitigation: jsdoc on each deprecated helper names the async replacement directly; CHANGELOG entry highlights the shutdown-correctness rationale.
- Risk: `Promise.all` in `stopSharedClientAsync` rejects on the first failing per-client persist.
  - Mitigation: Inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)`, so `stopAndPersist()` resolves under normal conditions; behavior matches `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")`.

---

target.title: fix(zalouser): surface send errors and recover from oversized style payloads
target.body:
## TL;DR

Zalo delivery failures were completely invisible: the monitor never checked send results, error codes were lost, and messages silently disappeared when styled payloads exceeded Zalo's size limit.

---

## Problem

Three compounding gaps caused messages to vanish without any log trace:

1. `sendZaloTextMessage` caught errors but discarded the numeric `error_code` from Zalo's response — callers could not distinguish a transient network error from a Zalo-specific rejection.
2. The `sendText` and `sendMedia` callbacks in the monitor called `sendMessageZalouser` without checking the returned result, so `ok=false` responses were silently dropped.
3. Zalo rejects messages with `error_code=112` when the combined params payload (message text + `textProperties` JSON for styling) exceeds its server-side size limit. Empirically: a ~1420-char Vietnamese message combined with 56+ style ranges (~1871 chars of JSON) triggers this. The message was never delivered and no error appeared in logs.

---

## Invariant

> When Zalo rejects a styled message with error code 112, the message text must still be delivered (without styles), and the failure must be logged if delivery ultimately fails.

---

## What this PR does

- Adds `errorCode?: number` to `ZaloSendResult` so callers can branch on specific Zalo error codes
- Enhances `toErrorMessage` to include the numeric code in the string so it appears in logs
- Extracts the numeric error code from caught errors in `sendZaloTextMessage` and attaches it to the result
- Adds a retry-without-styles path in `sendMessageZalouser` on error code 112: if the styled send fails with 112, the message is re-sent as plain text
- Makes `sendText` and `sendMedia` in the monitor check `result.ok` and emit a structured error log when delivery fails

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Long bot responses with markdown formatting silently failed to reach Zalo users. The bot appeared to respond on the web UI but nothing arrived in the chat.
- **System impact**: Failures were completely unobservable — no logs, no error propagation — making debugging impossible without adding instrumentation manually.

---

## Safety

- No change to message delivery path when everything succeeds
- The retry only triggers on the specific error code 112 with styled content; all other errors are forwarded unchanged
- Existing tests unchanged; 3 new regression tests added

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: retries without styles on error code 112
- [x] New test: propagates error when retry also fails
- [x] New test: does not retry when there are no styles

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: [codex] Add plugin task lifecycle API
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Plugins can inspect task runs today, but they do not have a narrow public SDK path for creating a plugin-owned task run, reporting progress, and finalizing the run while preserving owner/session scoping. That leaves plugin task lifecycle behavior either unavailable or tied to internal core task APIs that are not safe for third-party plugin contracts.

This PR adds a plugin-safe task run lifecycle API under `api.runtime.tasks.runs.bindSession(...).lifecycle` / `fromToolContext(...).lifecycle`. Plugin-created records are stored as existing `cli` task runtime rows with a required plugin-namespaced `taskKind`, and task creation identity now includes `taskKind` so same-owner/same-run records with different plugin task kinds stay separated.

## Summary

- Adds a plugin-safe task run lifecycle API under `api.runtime.tasks.runs.bindSession(...).lifecycle` / `fromToolContext(...).lifecycle`.
- Creates plugin-owned records as existing `cli` task runtime rows with required plugin-namespaced `taskKind`.
- Adds owner-scoped exact create/progress/finalize behavior and exposes `taskKind` in task run DTOs.
- Documents plugin-owned task records and SDK lifecycle usage.

## Evidence

Focused validation:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose

Tests  71 passed (71)
```

```text
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

```

Additional checks run:

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`

Redacted real runtime lifecycle proof at PR head `95cedbf4558905e68f351613f71fe7640df5b69e`, using `createRuntimeTaskRuns().bindSession(...).lifecycle` with an isolated temp state dir:

```text
$ pnpm tsx --eval '<runtime lifecycle create/progress/finalize proof script>'
{
  "created": {

    "sessionKey": "agent:proof:telegram:group:redacted",

  },

  },

  },

    "firstTaskKind": "proof-plugin.session",

```

This proves create, progress, finalize, same-kind reuse, and different-`taskKind` separation through the public plugin runtime lifecycle API.

## Known Base Failure

target.title: fix(qqbot): persist group inbound delivery route for announce target resolution
target.body:
## Summary

The `event.type !== "c2c"` guard at `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts:444` allows `dm` (direct message) events to also persist route via `updateLastRoute`, because `event.type` can be `"dm"` and `"dm" !== "c2c"` evaluates to `true`. This means QQ Bot direct messages unintentionally overwrite the session deliveryContext, which can cause group announce routing to target the DM conversation instead of the correct group.

Change the guard from `event.type !== "c2c"` to `event.type === "group" || event.type === "guild"`, explicitly gating route persistence to only group and guild turns. DM events (both `c2c` and `dm` types) are unconditionally excluded.

Fixes #94785

---

## What Problem This Solves (可选)

**Problem**: QQ Bot group announce delivery fails in multi-group scenarios because `updateLastRoute` incorrectly persists after non-group events (including `dm` type DMs), overwriting the session deliveryContext and causing downstream announce target resolution to route to the wrong conversation.

**Why This Change Was Made**: The original `!== "c2c"` negative-match is semantically wrong — it only excludes `c2c` but allows `dm` and any future non-group event type through. A positive-match (`=== "group" || === "guild"`) is the correct approach: only explicitly named group conversation types should trigger route persistence. This matches the pattern used in Discord (`isDirectMessage`), iMessage (`!decision.isGroup`), LINE (`!params.source.isGroup`), and Mattermost (`kind === "direct"`).

**User Impact**: Group announce messages (cron job completion, subagent emit, sessions_send) will now consistently route to the correct group conversation instead of falling back to key-parsed heuristics that may resolve to the wrong group. No impact on DM conversations.

---

## Linked context (可选)

- **Issue**: #94785
- **In scope**: Route persistence guard condition in `dispatchOutbound`; 4 new test cases for c2c/dm/group/guild event types
- **Out of scope**: Changes to announce message creation, Gateway session management, npm dependencies, or other QQ Bot dispatch paths

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gate QQ Bot route persistence to group and guild event types only, excluding c2c and dm direct messages.

**Real environment tested**: Linux 6.8.0-124-generic / OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (151575f) / Node v25.9.0 / DeepSeek V4 Flash model

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# Start Gateway from worktree with the fix
cd extensions/qqbot
DEEPSEEK_API_KEY="sk-***" pnpm dev gateway

# Send c2c (private) message via QQ Bot
# Send group message via QQ Bot

# Monitor route-persistence logs
cat /tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-06-30.log | grep route-persistence
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
# c2c private message — updateLastRoute correctly skipped
23:35:54 [qqbot] Processing message (type=c2c): "你好 这是一条私聊消息测试"
23:35:54 [qqbot] [route-persistence] inbound event.type=c2c isGroupChat=false -> omitting updateLastRoute
23:35:56 model-fetch: deepseek-v4-flash status=200 (145ms)
23:35:58 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

# Group message — updateLastRoute correctly applied
23:35:39 [qqbot] Processing message (type=group): "你好你是谁"
23:35:39 [qqbot] [route-persistence] inbound event.type=group isGroupChat=true -> applying updateLastRoute
23:35:40 model-fetch: deepseek-v4-flash status=200 (176ms)
23:35:42 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk (46/46 chars) with 0 HTTP images (group)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Both c2c and group messages are processed correctly. The `[route-persistence]` log confirms: `event.type=c2c` → `omitting updateLastRoute`, `event.type=group` → `applying updateLastRoute`. Both types successfully receive model replies.

**What was not tested**: QQ Bot `guild` event type (no guild access available). `dm` event type (QQ Bot API does not emit `dm` type; the fix covers it via unit tests). Real-world announce delivery scenario (requires cron job setup with specific session state).

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
   Duration  19.74s
```

4 new focused test cases covering all event types:
- `skips updateLastRoute for c2c events` ✅
- `skips updateLastRoute for dm events` ✅
- `persists updateLastRoute for group events` ✅
- `persists updateLastRoute for guild events` ✅

All 23 existing tests continue to pass — no regression.

### Integration / E2E

Real Gateway E2E test performed with live QQ Bot connection (appId=1903562876). Both c2c and group message paths exercised with DeepSeek V4 Flash model. Full route-persistence decision recorded in Gateway logs (see After-fix evidence above).

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — the new guard is strictly more restrictive (excludes `dm` events that were incorrectly allowed before)
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 27 unit tests pass + real Gateway E2E with QQ Bot
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [x] All CI checks passed

<!-- RF-001: Gate route persistence to group and guild turns — fixed by changing condition to positive-match -->
<!-- RF-002: Add focused QQ Bot tests for group/guild route writes and c2c/dm omissions — fixed by adding 4 new test cases -->
<!-- RF-003: Needs stronger real behavior proof — fixed by adding real Gateway logs showing both c2c and group message paths -->

target.title: feat(durable): add opt-in agent coordination kernel
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw already has strong surfaces for sessions, background tasks, Task Flow, Workboard, subagents, and Gateway channels, but long-running multi-agent work can still become hard to reason about when it spans many steps or branches. The concrete failure mode this PR targets is coordinator silence: a parent turn starts child/subagent work, one child is still running or fails, the gateway restarts, and the operator no longer has a bounded durable projection of which run, child, step, or recovery state is open.

This PR adds an opt-in native durable coordination kernel for that missing layer. It is intentionally not a general-purpose Temporal/Restate clone and does not replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. It gives those existing surfaces a shared local-first ledger for coordination identity, child links, fan-in readiness, restart recovery markers, and read-only observability.

Most useful validation evidence is below: focused regression tests cover the two ClawSweeper P1 state bugs, typecheck/build/guardrails pass, isolated enabled-runtime proof verifies Gateway projection and fan-in behavior, schema upgrade smoke preserves existing data, and a real OpenClaw A instance was updated to this branch and restarted with durable workflows enabled.

## Summary

This replaces the cumulative implementation stack from #97468, #97469, and #97476 with one self-contained vertical slice for agent review.

It adds an opt-in native durable agent coordination kernel that records:

- workflow runs, steps, events, refs, timers, signals, and child links;
- agent-turn lifecycle checkpoints;
- read-only Gateway coordination projection;
- CLI inspection helpers;
- subagent child-run recording and fan-in state.

The goal is not to replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. This PR gives those surfaces a shared, bounded coordination ledger for restart-safe multi-agent work.

## Reviewer Feedback Addressed

- **No sidecar DB by default.** Durable tables live in the canonical shared state database at `state/openclaw.sqlite` via `resolveOpenClawStateSqlitePath(...)`.
- **Shared schema lifecycle.** Durable tables are added to `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql` and regenerated schema artifacts.
- **Disabled by default.** Runtime hooks and Gateway reads require `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS` to be explicitly enabled.
- **Disabled read is non-mutating.** `durable.coordination.get` returns disabled without opening/creating durable state; this has a regression test.
- **No raw subagent task persistence.** Subagent coordination metadata stores ids, bounded labels/names, and `taskHash`; it no longer stores the raw `task` body.
- **No ledger-only operator controls.** The initial CLI surface is read-only inspection; cancel/retry/resume/signal write controls were removed until they can route through the live task/agent runtime contract.
- **Active parent binding.** `sessions_spawn` now passes the requester run id into the durable subagent mirror, and the bridge falls back to newest same-session parent only when older call paths do not provide a run id.
- **Fan-in reopen.** Registering a later child now reconciles fan-in immediately and clears stale `completedAt` when a terminal fan-in step returns to `waiting_child`.

## User Impact

This targets the class of bugs where long-running or branching agent work becomes silent or hard to recover:

- parent agent waits on subagents but cannot tell which child is still open;
- one failed child should not block unrelated sibling completion;
- gateway restart should not erase where a coordinated run was;
- operators need a bounded timeline/projection instead of scraping sessions or plugin state.

## Scope Boundaries

- Feature is opt-in and experimental.
- Normal chat turns are unchanged while the flag is off.
- This does not add CLI write controls, retention/compaction, Postgres, or multi-worker leases.
- This does not change Task Flow or Workboard semantics yet; it provides primitives they can project onto later.

## Evidence

Local proof environment: macOS local worktree, Node v25.8.1, commit `4664e48f67e76f3f488b2f9e2fade3e54183c588`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/var/folders/.../T` except for the OpenClaw A runtime smoke below.

| Reviewer ask | Command / proof | Observed result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parent-run association bug | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable/subagent.test.ts src/durable/fan-in.test.ts` | 2 files, 7 tests passed. New regression verifies same-session overlap links children to requester run id and fallback selects newest parent. |
| Fan-in reopen bug | Same durable test command | New regression verifies terminal fan-in step reopens to `waiting_child`, clears `completedAt`, and parent returns to waiting while later child runs. |
| `sessions_spawn` plumbing | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts` | 1 file, 53 tests passed. |
| Subagent lifecycle compatibility | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts` | 1 file, 8 tests passed. |
| Typecheck | `npm run tsgo:core` | Passed. |
| SQLite/Kysely guardrail | `node scripts/check-kysely-guardrails.mjs` | `Kysely guardrails OK`. |
| Format / whitespace | `npm run format:check -- <8 touched files>` and `git diff --check` | Passed. |
| Build | `npm run build` | Passed; generated runtime/UI/plugin artifacts built successfully. |
| Enabled agent turn + subagent fan-in + CLI/Gateway projection | `node --import tsx -e '<isolated durable proof script>'` | Created 3 runs, 4 steps, 15 events. Waiting projection: `waiting_child`, `waitingReason: child`, children `{ total: 2, running: 1, succeeded: 1, terminal: 1, open: 1 }`. Final projection: `succeeded/terminal`, children `{ total: 2, succeeded: 2, terminal: 2, open: 0 }`. |
| Gateway `durable.coordination.get` enabled/disabled | Same proof script calling the Gateway handler | Enabled response `ok: true`, projection children succeeded/open counts correct. Disabled response `ok: false`, `INVALID_REQUEST`, `Durable workflows are disabled.` |
| Redaction / bounded payloads | Same proof script inspected parent timeline payloads | `containsRawPrompt: false`, `containsRawTask: false`; prompt/task bodies are not stored raw in parent timeline payloads. |
| Fresh schema creation | Same proof script on empty isolated state | `state/openclaw.sqlite` created with durable stats `{ runs: 3, events: 15, steps: 4, openRuns: 0 }`; `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Upgrade schema smoke | Created DB from `origin/main:src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`, inserted sentinel `diagnostic_events` row, then opened durable store on this branch | Before durable tables: `[]`. After: 7 durable tables (`runs`, `events`, `steps`, `refs`, `links`, `timers`, `signals`). Sentinel row preserved. `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Real local OpenClaw A update/restart smoke | LaunchAgent points to `/Users/avo/aicos/providers/openclaw/worktrees/openclaw-x-durable-core/openclaw.mjs` with `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS=1`; `./runtime/supervisor/launchd/manage-openclaw-instance.sh local-a restart`; then health/stats/agent checks | Health returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`. Logs show `gateway ready`, `[durable/workflows] recorded durable gateway startup`, `[durable/recovery] started durable recovery worker`, Discord bot probe resolved `@bo`, and channel `bo-main` resolved. Existing agents list still includes `bo` plus configured worker agents. Durable stats on existing local-a state returned runs/events without losing old config. |

Proof event sequence from the enabled run:

```text
agent.turn.received
agent.turn.running
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.ready
agent.turn.succeeded
```

Known unrelated local-a warning during runtime smoke: the configured Telegram token is unauthorized and the gateway auth mode is explicitly `none`; both predate this durable slice and do not affect the Discord Bo/durable startup path checked above.

## Follow-Up PRs If This Boundary Is Accepted

- Live Gateway restart proof on a running OpenClaw instance, plus any bug fixes found there.
- Retention/compaction policy for durable events.
- Task Flow and Workboard projections.
- Multi-worker lease contention tests.

## 2026-06-30 Update: Schema Lifecycle And Restart Proof

Additional durable-core proof has been added on top of the earlier implementation slice:

- explicit durable schema version tracking via `durable_schema_migrations`;
- future-schema guard before durable workflow DDL, indexes, or column backfills;
- regression proving a future-version DB is rejected without mutating durable workflow tables;
- regression proving durable tables can be added to released pre-durable shared-state shapes while preserving existing rows;

target.title: fix(gateway): keep Android node surfaces usable after setup-code pairing
target.body:
Related: #87058
Related: #97967

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users pairing the Android app as a node through setup-code/device pairing could end up with a paired node whose safe declared command surface was collapsed to empty, leaving Android commands like `device.info` unavailable and `node.invoke` failing with "node did not declare any supported commands."

This covers the Android node-surface half of #87058. The separate Android onboarding connect-nonce retry race remains out of scope.

## Why This Change Was Made

Android WebSocket nodes already use device pairing as the durable approved `node` role contract, while `node.pair.*` is a separate surface store that does not gate WebSocket connect. For first-time first-party Android node connects, the gateway now uses the already-normalized Android declaration as the effective live surface after the existing platform allowlist has removed non-default or unsafe commands.

The shortcut is intentionally narrow: it requires `openclaw-android`, `node` mode, normalized Android platform metadata, and normalized Android device family. Existing paired node records, including explicit empty command approvals, keep the previous approval-intersection behavior and still require reapproval for upgrades.

## User Impact

Android users who pair through setup-code/device pairing can connect a usable node surface immediately for platform-safe commands such as `device.info` and `contacts.search`, instead of seeing a paired node that cannot invoke any declared command.

Non-Android node hosts and Android nodes with an existing approved surface keep their existing approval boundaries. Dangerous commands such as `camera.snap`, `screen.record`, and `sms.search` remain gated by the existing `gateway.nodes.allowCommands` policy.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the code change and verified the approval-boundary behavior.

Setup-code runtime proof:

- Added `qr setup code Android node exposes safe commands and handles node.invoke` in `src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.suite.ts`.
- The test uses a setup-code bootstrap token, connects a first-party `openclaw-android` node with Android metadata, then reconnects the same device identity with the bounded operator handoff token returned by bootstrap.
- It verifies `node.list` reports the Android node connected and paired with non-empty safe commands `contacts.search` and `device.info`.
- It also verifies `camera.snap` is filtered out by the existing platform allowlist and then completes a real gateway `node.invoke device.info` round trip over WebSocket through `node.invoke.request` and `node.invoke.result`.

Local proof:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts -- -t "qr setup code Android node exposes safe commands and handles node.invoke"` - passed (2 files, 2 tests; 72 skipped)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-command-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts` - passed (10 files, 186 tests)
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.ts src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-command-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.suite.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main --head HEAD` - passed
- `hook=$(git rev-parse --git-path hooks/pre-commit); "$hook"` - passed
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean after addressing one accepted finding; clean again after adding the setup-code runtime proof

Review note:

- Autoreview initially flagged that auto-adopting existing empty paired-node records could silently upgrade an explicit commandless approval. This PR now keeps existing paired records on the explicit reapproval path and adds regression coverage for that case.

Not run:

- Physical Android hardware verification. The PR now includes packaged gateway setup-code proof for the bootstrap-to-`node.invoke` path; #87058/#97967 contain live Android reproduction evidence for the empty-surface symptom.

target.title: fix: align gateway agent timeout default
target.body:
Closes #98180

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users copying generic agent config examples could accidentally cap normal agent runs at 10 minutes even though OpenClaw's runtime default is 48 hours when `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` is omitted.

## Why This Change Was Made

The generic docs/examples now omit `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` and explicitly explain that omitted values use the `172800` second runtime default. The gateway-backed `openclaw agent` path now preserves omitted CLI timeouts as server-owned instead of carrying a separate `600` second RPC override. Explicit `--timeout` remains an override. Heartbeat-specific 600 second caps are left unchanged because they are intentionally bounded and already documented as heartbeat behavior.

## User Impact

Users and operators can copy the general agent examples without silently shortening whole-agent runtime to 10 minutes. Gateway operators keep control of server-side `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` when CLI callers omit `--timeout`; callers can still set an explicit per-run timeout with `--timeout <seconds>`.

## Evidence

- `pnpm docs:list >/tmp/openclaw-timeout-docs-list.txt`
- `pnpm docs:list >/tmp/openclaw-timeout-docs-list-2.txt`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/gateway/configuration-examples.md docs/cli/agent.md`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/gateway/configuration-examples.md docs/cli/agent.md`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/timeout.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent-turn.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts` (70 agent-via-gateway tests + 16 CLI registration tests passed)
- CLI help proof: `pnpm exec tsx src/index.ts agent --help | rg -n -A2 -- "--timeout"` prints `Override agent command timeout (seconds, config value or runtime default 172800)`.
- Gateway dispatch regression proof in `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`: omitted `--timeout` does not serialize `params.timeout` and uses a 172830000ms client wait ceiling; explicit `--timeout 600` serializes `params.timeout: 600` and uses a 630000ms client wait ceiling.

AI-assisted PR. I understand the change: it removes generic 600s agent-runtime examples, documents the 48h runtime default, and keeps omitted gateway CLI timeouts server-owned while preserving explicit timeout overrides.

## Real Gateway-Backed Timeout Proof

Ran against an isolated temporary OpenClaw gateway, not the live gateway. The gateway config set `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 123` to prove omitted CLI timeout remains server-owned. A local WebSocket proxy only recorded the outbound CLI RPC frame and forwarded it to the real gateway; temp ports were redacted. The command used `/new` so the real `agent` gateway method completed without calling a model.

```json
{
  "gateway": {
    "isolated": true,
    "target": "127.0.0.1:<temp>",
    "proxy": "127.0.0.1:<temp>",
    "serverDefaultTimeoutSeconds": 123
  },
  "commands": {
    "omittedExit": 0,
    "explicitExit": 0,
    "omittedStderr": "",
    "explicitStderr": ""
  },
  "agentRpcFrames": [
    {
      "index": 1,
      "message": "/new",
      "sessionKey": "agent:main:timeout-proof-omitted",
      "hasTimeout": false,
      "paramsKeys": [
        "agentId",
        "cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd",
        "deliver",
        "idempotencyKey",
        "message",
        "sessionKey"
      ]
    },
    {
      "index": 2,
      "message": "/new",
      "sessionKey": "agent:main:timeout-proof-explicit",
      "hasTimeout": true,
      "timeout": 600,
      "paramsKeys": [
        "agentId",
        "cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd",
        "deliver",
        "idempotencyKey",
        "message",
        "sessionKey",
        "timeout"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Interpretation: current PR head preserves omitted `--timeout` as no `params.timeout` in the real CLI-to-gateway request, while explicit `--timeout 600` still serializes `params.timeout: 600`. Both calls were accepted by the actual gateway-backed `agent` method and returned `status: ok` reset acknowledgements.

Compatibility note: this intentionally changes omitted gateway CLI calls from the old local 600-second fallback to server-owned/default timeout behavior. Explicit `--timeout` remains the per-run override path.

target.title: fix(status): use legacy Codex OAuth profiles for OpenAI usage
target.body:
## Summary
- map legacy `openai-codex` OAuth/token profiles to the canonical `openai` usage provider
- let provider usage auth resolution try `openai-codex:*` credentials when collecting `openai` ChatGPT usage, including plugins that explicitly request `resolveOAuthToken({ provider: "openai" })`
- keep API keys excluded from ChatGPT usage auth

## Context
After the OpenAI/Codex provider migration, a user can still have a healthy legacy `openai-codex:*` OAuth profile while `openclaw status --usage` now asks the usage collector for provider `openai`. In that state the ChatGPT WHAM usage endpoint works with the stored token, but `openclaw status --usage --json` reports `usage.providers: []` / `Usage: no provider usage available`.

This preserves the new canonical `openai` usage provider while making usage auth backward-compatible with existing `openai-codex` OAuth/token profiles until users run doctor/migration.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw status --usage` returned no provider usage when the only live ChatGPT OAuth profile was stored under the legacy `openai-codex:*` provider id.
- Real environment tested: Mac mini running installed OpenClaw 2026.6.1 with the real main-agent profile store; the profile id/provider was inspected with secrets redacted (`openai-codex:*`, OAuth, account id present).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the local fallback command against the same real profile after resolving the legacy `openai-codex` OAuth token to the canonical OpenAI usage endpoint: `/Users/cyberowl/.openclaw/workspace/scripts/codex-usage-status.sh`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the real setup:

```text
Usage:
5h: 94% left · used 6% · reset 04.06.2026, 23:00
Week: 98% left · used 2% · reset 11.06.2026, 05:52
Plan: pro
Source: direct ChatGPT WHAM fallback
```

- Observed result after fix: The same real `openai-codex:*` OAuth profile produced live ChatGPT WHAM usage windows for 5h and Week instead of `usage.providers: []`. The branch makes the built-in usage auth resolver use that legacy profile for canonical `openai` usage, including the plugin path that explicitly asks for `resolveOAuthToken({ provider: "openai" })`.
- What was not tested: Not tested against a second real machine with a migrated SQLite `openai:*` profile; the existing behavior for canonical profiles is covered by current paths and the added regression tests focus on the legacy profile compatibility.

## Tests
- `CI=true pnpm vitest run src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.auth.normalizes-keys.test.ts`
